externalText: The Teal Book The Teal Book provides guidance that enables practitioners and teams to direct and manage portfolios, programmes and projects in government; ensuring the successful and timely delivery of government policy and business objectives. Published: 01 April 2025 Version: 2.0 (See all updates) Search Teal Book Search The Teal Book or View all contents What is The Teal Book? The Teal Book builds on the expectations of the Government Functional Standard for Project Delivery and serves as the core and definitive reference on how project delivery should be done in government. The Teal Book provides: an overview of project delivery in government and how it is governed, assured and structured a description of how project delivery operates in different sectors descriptions of the practices and techniques involved in directing and managing a portfolio, programme or project descriptions of the activities and techniques involved in planning and controlling the work descriptions of the activities and techniques involved in delivering the solution Updates 1 April 2025 Version 2.0 of The Teal Book replaces version 1.0 and has the same purpose, scope and intent. The main changes relate to general enhancements derived from feedback, use and changes to government policy. This version still covers the technical practices of portfolio, programme and project management, planning and control, and solution delivery in the context of the UK Government, as set by the Government Functional Standard for Project Delivery. 29 July 2024 First major version of The Teal Book published Back to top Cover of the programme and project data standard, and Adopting the programme and project data standard Standard Programme and project data standard Setting the expectation for the creation of programme and project data across government. News New feasibility studies guidance strengthens decision-making A feasibility study is a structured assessment to see whether a proposed change can realistically be delivered. Blog Building government project delivery together My vision is for a dynamic profession, recognised globally for its expertise and commitment to outcomes. News Published: 01 April 2026 Government strengthens project delivery and accountability Published: 01 April 2026 New feasibility studies guidance strengthens early decision-making Published: 01 April 2026 New GRIP requirements for major projects View all news Blogs Author: Owen Kennedy Published: 28 May 2026 Reimagining assurance: reflections from the PMI Global Executive Council Author: William Emmett Published: 01 May 2026 Senior leaders reflect on the importance of The Teal Book Author: Becky Wood Published: 01 April 2026 Building government project delivery together View all blogs Back to top News Government Project Delivery wins two awards for website excellence Published: 06 November 2024 Last modified: 03 March 2025 Government Project Delivery is proud to announce that it has been honoured at two prestigious digital media industry awards this month for the Government Project Delivery Hub. These awards are: The Digital Impact Awards (Europe), winning bronze for creativity, strategic vision and ability to harness the power of digital to engage audiences, connect workforces and communicate crucial information. The Web Excellence Awards (International), winning the e-Government category, for excellence in showcasing commitment to innovation and superior performance in the digital space Both the Digital Impact Awards and the Web Excellence Awards provide a platform to acknowledge and promote excellence in web development and digital innovation and this year saw an impressive array of talent from across Europe and around the world. Other winners at the Digital Impact Awards included PwC in the use of digital from the professional services sector, EE (BT Group) for digital accessibility and Electronic Arts for the use of existing social media platforms. At the Web Excellence Awards winners included the NATO Innovation Fund for the digital implementation of their identity, The Center for Arts in the United States of America for how they present live events and performances online, and Too Good To Go for performance improvements in their website. Government Project Delivery and Connect Internet Solutions at the Digital Impact Awards (Left to right: Alice Cipolat, Communications and Engagement Manager; Miren Shah, Skills Data Manager; William Emmett, Development Lead and Service Manager; Owen Kennedy, Deputy Director and Service Owner; Jay Chauhan, Communications Lead; Filipe Ribeiro, Connect Internet Solutions; David Foster, Connect Internet Solutions) The Hub was launched in May 2022 as the digital centre for project delivery. Since its launch, its goals have expanded significantly. Now, with approximately 12,000 users, it serves as the primary location for accessing all government project delivery services, resources, and community engagement. Owen Kennedy, Deputy Director and Service Owner for the Government Project Delivery Hub, stated, “We are excited to receive this award, which recognises the hard work of our small service team in prioritising the interests, needs, and priorities of our users. We always strive to find solutions that make it easier for professionals to achieve successful project outcomes. We would like to thank all our users and our supplier, Connect Internet Solutions, for their valuable feedback and suggestions, which help us continuously improve the services and information on our website.” The Hub team has put in significant effort to develop a more user-friendly platform that caters to the needs of project delivery professionals in government and the broader public sector. This new approach makes it easier to access the information they require, thanks to innovative indexing across an extensive library of enhanced content. William Emmett, Function Development Lead and Service Manager, said “Being recognised as a winner at both the Digital Impact Awards and the Web Excellence Awards not only highlights our achievements but also motivates us to continue raising the bar for members of the government project delivery profession.” The team continues to welcome feedback from users, either by letting them know whether you find content useful or not at the bottom of pages, or on the content of The Teal Book via the feedback tool. About the Digital Impact Awards The Digital Impact Awards celebrate the very best in corporate digital communications from around the world. Setting an industry-wide benchmark for excellence in digital stakeholder engagement, the awards honour the most innovative, strategic and impactful work in the corporate communications space. About the Web Excellence Awards The Web Excellence Awards is a leading international competition celebrating the best in web development and digital innovation. Established to recognise and promote excellence across the digital industry, the Web Excellence Awards provide a platform for innovation and creativity to be acknowledged globally. News Published: 01 April 2026 Government strengthens project delivery and accountability Published: 01 April 2026 New feasibility studies guidance strengthens early decision-making Published: 01 April 2026 New GRIP requirements for major projects View all news Back to top News The Teal Book named HM Treasury Innovation of the Year 2025 Published: 13 January 2026 Last modified: 13 January 2026 The Teal Book, the government’s code of practice for portfolio, programme and project delivery, is HM Treasury’s Innovation of the Year 2025. Developed by Government Project Delivery, The Teal Book explains how organisations can meet the project delivery functional standard in a way that works in government delivery environments. It turns expectations into practical steps that teams can apply across their environments. This recognition from HM Treasury follows growing interest from the wider project delivery community in the UK and overseas. The Teal Book’s design, content and structure have established it as a dynamic reference source. What the award recognises The Innovation of the Year award recognises The Teal Book for providing: a clear, end-to-end explanation of what good project delivery looks like across government practical content that supports teams at different levels of maturity a single place that connects all guidance across functions that supports government project delivery a model for how central guidance can be continually improved based on user feedback For professionals working in Government Project Delivery, this means it is easier to: understand how the functional standard applies to their work align local frameworks and tools with cross-government expectations access authoritative guidance from a single source Growing cross-government and international interest The Teal Book was created with extensive input from departments, arm’s length bodies and across the function and profession. This collaborative approach is reflected in how it is being used across government, with thousands of users engaging with the guidance, providing feedback and helping to prioritise future improvements. While The Teal Book was developed for UK central government project delivery teams, its approach and structure are attracting wider attention from: other government functions who are exploring how the model could support their own standards and guidance overseas public sector organisations, professional bodies and academic institutions have requested demonstrations and case studies online project delivery communities have been sharing and discussing The Teal Book, generating interest well beyond its original audience This external engagement is helping to position the UK’s project delivery function as a reference point for modern public sector practice. What’s next The central Government Project Delivery function team is continuing to develop The Teal Book. Feedback from the website and real examples from across government are already influencing the next version. This keeps The Teal Book as a living resource that improves project delivery in government while showcasing UK practice to international partners and suppliers. As highlighted in the Think Digital Partners feature, the focus now turns to helping organisations adopt The Teal Book at scale and connecting it closely with assurance, capability building and performance measures. For colleagues who prefer something they can keep on their desk or take into a meeting room, there will be a welcome update soon – designed to complement, not replace, the digital-first service. Back to top About Government Project Delivery We are the people, work and development of project delivery across government. From: Government Project Delivery Published: 10 February 2022 Last modified: 22 October 2025 (See all updates) Centre of expertise for project delivery in government Government Project Delivery is the function and profession for project delivery in the UK government. It is a collaborative organisation connecting the over 26,000 civil servants that deliver projects across the country. Government Project Delivery is hosted by the National Infrastructure and Service Transformation Authority, reporting to the Cabinet Office and HM Treasury. About project delivery The UK government delivers some of the most complex and innovative projects and programmes in the world. Big or small, all government projects, programmes and portfolios, and the public servants working within them, are part of Government Project Delivery. These projects, ranging from infrastructure and construction to military capability and digital transformation, are essential to improving public services, driving economic growth, and achieving mission-led government ambitious goals like net zero. About the project delivery function The project delivery function focuses on improving how government can best achieve its project delivery objectives by setting out consistent guides, shared learning and proven frameworks for delivery. The Government Functional Standard for Project Delivery sets out clear expectations and requirements for project delivery across government departments and clearly establishes the role of the government project delivery function. About the project delivery profession The project delivery profession involves making sure government has the right people with the right skills in project management to support the huge variety of portfolios, programmes and projects across the country. Resources and support Government Project Delivery brings together expertise and resources to support project delivery in government. One platform for project delivery Central to its vision is the development of a single, online platform where professionals can access standards, tools, and best practices. Building this library of knowledge and a network of professionals means project delivery professionals can deliver better projects, faster. The projectdeliery.gov.uk proposition sets out what the site is for and who can register. The roadmap describes what the website team are working on and planning to do. By working closely with industry, professional bodies, and academia, Government Project Delivery is building a strong network of expertise, ensuring that the UK remains at the forefront of project delivery innovation. Accreditation Accreditation provides an industry-aligned standard by which project delivery professionals working across government can be recognised with a ‘licence to practice’. Over 4,000 people across government have been accredited so far. Find out how to get accredited Building capability The Project Delivery Capability Framework (PDCF) describes the roles and competencies for all project delivery professionals and aspiring professionals across government. To support this, the Government Projects Academy has recommended training for project delivery professionals. It includes courses and development programmes to build practical skills and support career progression. Increasing the use of data and artificial intelligence (AI) Data and AI is being used across Government Project Delivery to improve how portfolios, programmes and projects are planned, managed and delivered. This work is guided by the functional strategy and the report ‘Data Analytics and AI in Government Project Delivery‘. Find out more about data and AI in project delivery Supporting delivery through clear, consistent guidance The Government Functional Standard for Project Delivery is the guiding document for how portfolios, programmes and projects should be delivered across government. The Teal Book provides detailed guidance to help teams meet the expectations set out in the standard. It supports good delivery by helping practitioners manage work in line with government policy and objectives. The publications library builds on this by offering guides, tools, templates and case studies. These resources support the standard and are being developed in line with Teal Book guidance. How Government Project Delivery is governed The governance structure of Government Project Delivery is a key aspect of its commitment to excellence and accountability. It is a multi-layered structure, designed to ensure that project delivery aligns with government policy, meets the needs of the British people, and is delivered efficiently and effectively. Central leadership Government Project Delivery is led by the Government Head of Function for Project Delivery and the Government Head of Profession for Project Delivery. Both of these roles are hosted by the National Infrastructure and Service Transformation Authority (NISTA) and work to ensure that project delivery is delivered effectively by people with the required skills and experience. Chief project delivery officers The chief project delivery officers are senior officials accountable for project delivery in their respective government organisations. They work with the Government Head of Function to ensure that project delivery aligns with the government’s priorities and is delivered effectively. Heads of profession The heads of profession are senior officials accountable for the development of project delivery professionals in their respective departments. They work with their chief project delivery officer and the Government Head of Profession to ensure that project delivery has the required capability and capacity. Key governance bodies Civil Service Board The Civil Service Board is the most senior decision-making body for the Civil Service. They are responsible for setting the overall strategic direction for the government function and profession models Projects Council The Projects Council brings together the chief project delivery officers from across government to discuss and address system-wide issues, share best practices, drive continuous improvement and own the overall strategic direction for Government Project Delivery Head of Profession Steering Group This steering group brings together heads of profession from across government to support Projects Council in discussing and addressing system-wide issues, share best practices, drive continuous improvement and the strategic direction for the function and profession Product Board The Product Board oversees the development and delivery of project delivery products, including standards, guidance, and tools, ensuring that they meet the strategic needs of the function and profession and are aligned with the government’s priorities Updates 10 February 2022 First published. 01 March 2025 Further details and information added about the governance and structure of Government Project Delivery. 16 April 2025 Government Project Delivery is now hosted by the National Infrastructure and Service Transformation Authority. 22 October 2025 More information added around the resources and support available, including: updating the number of people who have been accredited the Project Delivery Capability Framework has been updated new information around the work in data and AI in project delivery links to the updated Government Functional Standard for Project Delivery and The Teal Book Back to top Site policy Accessibility statement Find out how accessible this website is, report problems and request content in alternative formats From: Government Project Delivery Published: 21 April 2023 Last modified: 13 January 2026 Overview This statement applies to content published on the projectdelivery.gov.uk. It does not cover content on other websites linked from this site. For example, GOV.UK has its own accessibility statement. This website is run by the National Infrastructure and Service Transformation Authority in HM Treasury on behalf of Government Project Delivery. How you should be able to use this website We want as many people as possible to be able to use this website. You should be able to: change colours, contrast levels and fonts using browser or device settings zoom in up to 400% without text spilling off the screen navigate most of the website using a keyboard or speech recognition software listen to most of the website using a screen reader (including the most recent versions of JAWS, NVDA and VoiceOver) We aim to make the text as simple as possible to understand while maintaining accuracy and technical detail about project delivery. AbilityNet has advice on making your device easier to use if you have a disability. How accessible this website is Parts of this website are not fully accessible. For example: some pages and document attachments are not written in plain English some PDF documents are not fully accessible, but HTML alternatives are provided some figures have alternative text, but it does not always convey the full meaning some error messages are not clearly associated with form controls webinar recordings rely on auto-generated captions Each organisation that publishes content on projectdelivery.gov.uk is responsible for meeting accessibility regulations. We will update the statement when issues are fixed or when we expect them to be fixed. Feedback and contact information Tell us if you need information in a different format In your message, include: the web address (URL) of the content your email address and name the format you need – for example, plain text, braille, BSL, large print or an audio file Where content has been created by a government organisation and published on projectdelivery.gov.uk, you can request an accessible format from its library product page, there is a ‘Request an accessible format’ link to contact the organisation that published the document. You can also view Government Project Delivery’s accessible documents policy to report any problems or request documents in an alternative format. Reporting accessibility problems with this website If you find any problems that are not listed on this page or you think we’re not meeting the accessibility requirements, contact us. Enforcement procedure The Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC) is responsible for enforcing the Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) (No. 2) Accessibility Regulations 2018 (the ‘accessibility regulations’). If you contact us with a complaint and you’re not happy with our response contact the Equality Advisory and Support Service (EASS). Technical information about this website’s accessibility The National Infrastructure and Service Transformation Authority, on behalf of Government Project Delivery, is committed to making its websites accessible, in accordance with the Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) (No. 2) Accessibility Regulations 2018. Compliance status This website is partially compliant with the World Wide Web Consortium’s Web Content Accessibility Guidelines version 2.2 AA standard. Non-accessible content The content listed below is non-accessible for the following reasons. Non-compliance with the accessibility regulations Some images and figures have alternative text, but the description does not always convey the full meaning or references colours. This fails WCAG 2.2 success criterion 1.1.1 (non-text Content). Some PDF and Word documents have missing titles, metadata or required tagging. This fails WCAG 2.2 success criterion 2.4.2 (page titles) and WCAG 2.2 success criterion 1.3.1 (information and relationships). Webinar recording captions are automatically generated and may contain errors. This does not fully meet WCAG 2.2 success criterion 1.2.2 (captions – prerecorded). If you need an accurate transcript of a specific webinar recording, contact us and we can provide one. PDFs and non-HTML documents Some documents aren’t accessible because they: have missing or incomplete metadata don’t include text alternatives for images and figures don’t use a semantic structure (for example, headings and lists are not marked up) HTML versions of these documents are available from their library product page. Content that’s not within the scope of the accessibility regulations The accessibility regulations do not require us to fix PDFs or other documents published before 23 September 2018 if they’re not essential to providing our services. How we tested this website We use the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2 level A and level AA to test how accessible projectdelivery.gov.uk is and complete regular accessibility scans and audits. What we’re doing to improve accessibility We are working alongside government organisations to be fully compliant with WCAG 2.2 AA success criteria. To improve compliance, we: work with teams during content development to create accessible content from the start include document accessibility reviews as part of our publishing process build accessibility knowledge across content owners through ongoing support, guidance and training reviewing the accessibility of pages and sections when content updates are needed Preparation of this accessibility statement This statement was first prepared on 26 March 2022. It was last reviewed in January 2026. This website was last tested in June 2025 against the WCAG 2.2 AA standard. The departments and directorates of the Scottish, Welsh and the Northern Ireland civil service Crown Dependency governments Legislative bodies. From: Government Project Delivery Published: 21 April 2023 Last modified: 07 November 2025 (See all updates) How does projectdelivery.gov.uk use cookies? This policy is part of our wider privacy policy and you should read and understand both before agreeing to them. Change your cookies settings. View our privacy policy. What are cookies? Cookies are small text files which are transferred to your computer, mobile or tablet device when you visit a website or app. Why do we use cookies? We use cookies to do a few different things: To remember information about you, so you don’t have to give it to us multiple times. To keep you signed in To help us understand how people are using our services so we can make them better To help us personalise the site to you by remembering your preferences and settings, for example, your personal quicklinks. What are the types of cookies? Some cookies are always on when you visit us and you can’t turn them off unless you change your browser settings. We call these “necessary cookies”. We use them to make sure our digital services work correctly and are meeting our user’s needs and interests. We also use “analytical cookies” to allow us to understand how the site is being used so we can make improvements and make our user’s experience more enjoyable. You can switch these on or off at any time and you can always change your mind. We’ll only use them if you’ve agreed. There are also some cookies that come from other companies and services. These “third-party cookies” might track how you use different websites, including ours. For example, you might get a cookie when you play a YouTube or Vimeo video which we embed on our site. You can turn them off, but not through us. To view this third-party content, you first have to accept their specific terms and conditions. This includes their cookie policies, which we have no control over. But if you do not view this content, no third-party cookies are installed on your device. How long do cookies last? Some are erased when you close the browser you have used to visit the site. Others stay longer, sometimes forever, and are saved onto your device so that they are there when you come back. How do I control my cookies? Necessary cookies are always on when you visit us. On your first visit to us, we’ll tell you about our other types of cookies and ask you to choose which cookies we can use. You can always change your mind by going to your settings. Stopping all cookies might mean that you can’t access some of our services, or that some of them might not work properly for you. Another way to control cookies is in the settings on your device.
